name: AI Safety and Governance Separation Across Planes

Separate AI policy and governance (Control) from workflow execution (Application) and inference/compute (Data).

summary: Introduce a UI-facing boundary that provides a client-optimized, synchronous interface while bridging to asynchronous and/or multi-backend interactions behind the boundary.

terms.value: Introduce a UI-facing boundary that provides a client-optimized, synchronous interface while bridging to asynchronous and/or multi-backend interactions behind the boundary.
Make the UI-to-backend integration shape an explicit architectural choice so CAF can compile obligations and tasks for a UI-facing adapter boundary without assuming frameworks or protocols.
Applies when a user-facing UI (e.g., SPA) requires a stable synchronous interface, while the internal system interactions are asynchronous, multi-plane, or require aggregation/composition.
Directly coupling a UI to asynchronous plane interactions (events, workflows) or multiple backends forces UI complexity, leaks internal contracts, and makes evolution difficult.

Select a modeling framework that yields plane-aware boundaries, invariants, and test seams without prescribing domain-specific nouns.

Make domain modeling and boundary structure an explicit architectural choice so CAF can compile obligations and validation gates from pattern prose and pins while keeping solution content non-deterministic within rails.
Systems with non-trivial rules, permissions, and multi-plane concerns where correctness depends on explicit boundaries and stable interfaces.
Without an explicit modeling framework, CAF and workers tend to drift toward ad-hoc CRUD stubs and coupled modules; cross-plane designs become ambiguous and hard to validate.
ddd_clean_default: Domain-driven modeling with Clean Architecture style boundaries (recommended default for multi-plane SaaS).

active_record_mvc: MVC/Active Record style with framework-centric modeling; fastest scaffolding, weakest boundary guarantees.
custom: Custom framework (architect provides boundary rules and invariants).

name: MRAD-1 Idempotent Command Pattern
summary: Mobile-triggered state changes are retry-safe; duplicate submissions do not create duplicate effects.

terms.value: Mobile-triggered state changes are retry-safe; duplicate submissions do not create duplicate effects.
Make write operations safe under unreliable networks and client retries.
Mobile and constrained channels with intermittent connectivity and uncertain delivery outcomes.
Clients retry requests under ambiguity, causing duplicate effects without idempotency controls.
request_header: Idempotency key in a dedicated request header.
request_body_field: Idempotency key as an explicit field in the request body.
derived_from_resource_key: Idempotency derived from a stable resource key (no explicit client key).

name: MRAD-2 Resumable Workflow Pattern
summary: Long-running actions return durable identifiers and can be resumed/observed without restarting.

terms.value: Long-running actions return durable identifiers and can be resumed/observed without restarting.
Support reliable completion of long-running actions under intermittent client connectivity.
Mobile workflows where clients may disconnect, background, or lose connectivity mid-operation.
If progress depends on continuous connectivity, users must restart operations, creating poor UX and inconsistent state.
server_workflow_id: Server issues a stable workflow/execution id for resume/retry.
client_resume_token: Client holds an opaque resume token issued by the server.
checkpoint_step_id: Resume via explicit checkpoint/step identifiers with server-side state.

name: MRAD-3 Bounded Payload and Collection Pattern
summary: Mobile-facing APIs provide bounded response shapes via pagination and controlled expansions.

terms.value: MRAD-3 Bounded Payload and Collection Pattern
Mobile-facing APIs provide bounded response shapes via pagination and controlled expansions.
Prevent unbounded responses that degrade mobile reliability and performance.
Clients with constrained bandwidth/latency where large payloads cause timeouts and poor UX.
Unbounded collections and nested expansions cause unpredictable payload sizes and failure modes.
hard_limits_and_pagination: Hard payload limits with pagination for collections.
chunked_transfer: Chunked transfer with explicit part limits and assembly rules.
indirect_reference: Large payloads passed by reference (pointer/handle) with validation.

summary: Avoid chatty sequences on critical paths by using coarse-grained operations and server-side aggregation.

terms.value: Avoid chatty sequences on critical paths by using coarse-grained operations and server-side aggregation.
Reduce round trips and failure surfaces for constrained channels.
Mobile/voice channels where many small calls amplify latency and error probability.
Chatty interactions require many round trips, leading to poor performance and higher failure rates.
coarse_commands: Coarse-grained command endpoints that perform multi-step work.
batch_operations: Batch endpoints that accept/return collections in one call.

terms.value: Incrementally replace legacy behind a gateway
Modernize safely by incrementally replacing legacy functionality behind a stable interface.
Legacy systems with high risk of big-bang rewrite; you can route traffic by capability, user segment, or endpoint.
A full rewrite is risky and slow; the business still needs continuous delivery while modernization happens.

A modernization approach where a new system gradually takes over functionality from a legacy system by routing specific paths/features to new components. Over time the ‘strangler’ expands until the legacy system is fully retired, enabling incremental delivery and reduced migration risk.
Speed of replacement vs maintaining two systems; data consistency vs migration safety; clean boundaries vs messy legacy coupling.
Reduces rewrite risk and enables phased value delivery; temporary complexity (dual routing, dual data paths) until legacy is fully retired.
Identify “seams” (endpoints/modules). Use feature flags/traffic splitting. Ensure strong observability and clear decommission criteria per slice.
Routing specific endpoints from a monolith to new services; gradually moving UI flows to a new backend.
No cloud vendor or product selection implied by this pattern.
Verify that reduces rewrite risk and enables phased value delivery

name: Structured Logging
summary: Queryable logs with a consistent schema

terms.value: Queryable logs with a consistent schema
How will structured logging schemas be governed?
Make logs queryable and consistent by using a standard schema and fields.
Microservices and async flows where debugging spans many components and failures are partial.
Without consistent telemetry, issues are hard to detect, triage, and remediate quickly.

Logging in a machine-parseable format (often JSON) with consistent fields such as timestamp, request_id, user/tenant, severity, and component. Structured logs enable powerful querying and dashboards, but must avoid sensitive data and enforce schema discipline.
Signal quality vs cost/volume; cardinality vs queryability; instrumentation effort vs MTTR reduction.
Faster detection and lower MTTR; increased telemetry costs and governance requirements.
Standardize correlation IDs, structured logs, tracing, and SLOs; alert on user impact; sample intelligently.
Production incident response, performance tuning, auditing critical flows.
No cloud vendor or product selection implied by this pattern.

name: Timeout + Deadlines
summary: Bound latency; propagate deadlines downstream

terms.value: What timeout/deadline model should be used across calls?
Bound end-to-end latency by enforcing timeouts and propagating deadlines across calls.
Distributed systems with unreliable networks and variable load; async workflows and integrations.
Synchronous chaining and naive retries cause cascading failures, duplicate side effects, and poor elasticity under spikes.

A practice of enforcing maximum durations for requests and propagating a deadline budget across service calls. Timeouts prevent indefinite resource consumption and help systems fail fast; combined with cancellation and retries, they improve tail latency and overall stability.
Consistency vs availability; throughput vs ordering; retry aggressiveness vs dependency protection; simplicity vs correctness.
More resilient and scalable workflows; more components and operational complexity (queues, topics, retries, replays).
Design for at-least-once delivery, idempotency, backoff+jitter, DLQs, and clear ownership for replay/compensation.
Order/payment workflows, event streams, background processing, third-party integration pipelines.
No cloud vendor or product selection implied by this pattern.
Verify that timeouts prevent indefinite resource consumption and help systems fail fast

Synchronous chaining and naive retries cause cascading failures, duplicate side effects, and poor elasticity under spikes.

name: Write-Through Cache
summary: Cache updated on writes

terms.value: What write-through behavior should be used on writes?
Systems needing low-latency reads, scalable storage, and fit-for-purpose data models.
One-size-fits-all persistence and modeling leads to slow reads, complex queries, and scaling bottlenecks.

A caching strategy where writes go to the cache and the backing store in a single operation, keeping cache entries fresh. It simplifies reads and consistency, but can add write latency and requires well-defined behavior when either cache or store is unavailable.
Duplication vs performance; consistency vs speed; operational burden vs specialization benefits.
Better performance and clearer models; more data stores/projections to manage.
Start with simple caching/projections, measure hotspots, and introduce specialized stores/projections incrementally.
No cloud vendor or product selection implied by this pattern.

name: Zero Trust
summary: Verify identity/context; minimize trust in network

terms.value: Verify identity/context; minimize trust in network
What is the scope of zero-trust enforcement?
Assume the network is hostile; verify identity, device, and context for every request.
Systems handling sensitive data and multiple services/identities across networks and environments.
Implicit trust, broad permissions, and ad-hoc secret handling create high breach impact and compliance risk.

A security model that assumes no implicit trust based on network location. Every access request is continuously verified using strong identity, device and context signals, and least-privilege policies. It typically includes mTLS, conditional access, micro-segmentation, and continuous monitoring.
Security depth vs developer friction; least privilege vs operational agility; privacy vs usability.
Reduced blast radius and better compliance; requires governance, tooling, and ongoing hygiene.
Centralize identity, enforce least privilege, store secrets in a vault, keep services private by default, and automate policy checks.
Enterprise SaaS, healthcare/finance workloads, multi-tenant platforms.
No cloud vendor or product selection implied by this pattern.
